topic: cool climate conditions and wine style - acidity, alcohol, and aromatic character
trigger_keywords: vino de clima fresco frente a uno de clima cálido
mayor acidez, fruta menos madura, alcohol moderado
estilo de vino se obtiene en un clima marítimo fresco
clima favorece los aromas de pirazina en variedades como Cabernet Sauvignon
impacto principal de un clima fresco sobre los vinos tintos

steps.label: cause
steps.text: In cool climate regions, average growing season temperatures are low enough that grapes ripen slowly, often incompletely, and the growing season is shorter with risk of harvest interruption by autumn cold or rain.

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: Slow, cool ripening preserves the grape's natural malic and tartaric acid content because overnight temperatures do not sufficiently accelerate acid respiration. Sugar accumulation is limited, so potential alcohol levels remain lower. Aromatic compounds associated with underripeness - green apple, citrus, grass, herb - remain present alongside any ripe fruit that develops during warm spells.

steps.label: effect
steps.text: Wines from cool climates typically display high acidity, lower alcohol, lighter body, and primary fruit characteristics that lean toward green apple, citrus, and herbaceous notes. Tannins in reds may be more angular and astringent if grapes do not fully ripen.

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Cool climate explains high acidity, lower alcohol, and lighter body in the wine because slow ripening preserves natural acids and limits sugar accumulation. In SAT tasting terms, observing crisp acidity, low-medium alcohol, and citrus or herbaceous notes points toward a cool or marginal climate origin.

sat_relevance: Directly relevant to SAT climate inference. Cool climate markers (high acidity, lower alcohol, citrus/herbaceous notes) form a coherent cluster that supports a cool-region conclusion.

_meta.source_note: Inferred formative model - not derived from official WSET mark scheme or syllabus document
